lessons.body: println! formats values and writes exactly one line to stdout.
lessons.title: Output

lessons.body: let creates immutable bindings by default; mut makes rebinding through the same name explicit.
lessons.title: Bindings and mutability

lessons.body: Numeric types are explicit when inference is not enough; tuples group a fixed number of different values.
lessons.title: Numbers and tuples

lessons.body: String owns growable UTF-8 text; &str is a borrowed string slice into existing UTF-8 text.
lessons.title: Strings

lessons.body: if can produce a value, and loop forms such as for let you turn ranges or collections into accumulated results.
lessons.title: Control flow

lessons.body: Function signatures name parameter types and return types; the last expression can be the returned value.
lessons.title: Functions

lessons.body: A struct names related fields, and an impl block attaches methods and associated functions to that type.
lessons.title: Structs and impl

lessons.body: Enums model a closed set of variants, and match forces each variant to be handled deliberately.
lessons.title: Enums and match

lessons.body: Option<T> makes absence explicit, so code must handle Some(value) and None instead of assuming a value exists.
lessons.title: Option and if let

lessons.body: mod creates a namespace, pub exposes selected items, and use brings a path into local scope without changing ownership.
lessons.title: Modules and use

lessons.body: Coding-test Rust usually reads stdin as text, splits it, parses tokens once, and solves with typed values.
lessons.title: Input parsing

lessons.body: Vec<T> stores ordered values, while HashMap<K, V> stores lookups by key; entry is the usual counting API.
lessons.title: Vec and HashMap

lessons.body: Borrowed slices let functions read part of owned data without taking ownership of the whole value.
lessons.title: Borrowing and slices

lessons.body: Result<T, E> represents recoverable failure; the ? operator unwraps Ok or returns the Err to the caller.
lessons.title: Result and ?

lessons.body: Each owned value has one owner; moving transfers ownership, while borrowing lets code inspect data without taking it.
lessons.title: Ownership and borrowing

lessons.body: Iterators are lazy until consumed; closures let map, filter, and fold express local transformations.
lessons.title: Iterators and closures

lessons.body: Generics let one function or type work with many concrete types while preserving compile-time type checking.
lessons.title: Generics

lessons.body: Traits describe shared behavior, and bounds say which behavior a generic function is allowed to rely on.
lessons.title: Traits and bounds

lessons.body: Lifetime annotations describe relationships between borrowed values; they do not make any value live longer.
lessons.title: Lifetimes

lessons.body: Trait objects such as &dyn Trait allow values of different concrete types to be used through shared behavior.
lessons.title: Trait objects and dyn dispatch

lessons.body: Rust test functions use #[test] and assertion macros; normal code still needs small pure functions that tests can call.
lessons.title: Tests and assertions

lessons.body: Smart pointers such as Box<T> own data with pointer-like behavior and can place values on the heap.
lessons.title: Smart pointers

lessons.body: RefCell<T> checks borrow rules at runtime, allowing mutation through an immutable owner when the design requires it.
lessons.title: Interior mutability

lessons.body: thread::spawn moves work to another OS thread, and join waits for that thread's result.
lessons.title: Threads and join

lessons.body: Arc<T> shares ownership across threads, and Mutex<T> protects mutation so only one thread edits at a time.
lessons.title: Shared state with Arc and Mutex

lessons.body: async creates a Future that can pause at await points; executing futures needs a runtime or executor.
lessons.title: Async and await

lessons.body: macro_rules! matches token patterns at compile time and expands them into Rust code before type checking.
lessons.title: macro_rules!

lessons.body: unsafe enables operations the compiler cannot fully verify, but the programmer must still uphold Rust's safety rules.
lessons.title: Unsafe Rust

lessons.body: Cargo manages packages, dependencies, tests, and workspaces; workspace commands let related crates build together.
lessons.title: Cargo packages and workspaces
